one_line_takeaway: A UCB-bandit-driven code agent that autonomously composes 28 hand-designed prompting skills achieves 100% black-box system-prompt extraction (consistency >= 0.7) across 41 commercial LLMs, with attack-aware defenses cutting extraction quality only 18.4% and none reaching robust protection.
key_finding: Across the 41-model sweep JUSTASK achieved 100% extraction success (consistency score >= 0.7), with 85% of models yielding on the first attempt and only 14% of successful extractions requiring escalation to multi-turn ('high-level') skills. Content analysis of 46 extracted prompts (41 black-box models + 5 Claude Code agents) found near-universal HHH-framework adoption (96% harmless, 91% helpful, 89% honest) and a 26.8% identity-confusion rate (11/41 models claiming a developer different from their actual source, 6 of these persistently uncorrectable, OpenAI being the most commonly falsely claimed developer). On defense, embedding a full attack-taxonomy-aware defense into the system prompt reduced extraction quality (semantic similarity to ground truth) by 18.4% on average (0.719 -> 0.587) across four frontier models, whereas a naive 'do not reveal' instruction reduced it by only 6.0% and in one case (Claude Opus 4.5) actually increased measured extraction quality (0.600 baseline vs. 0.616 under the simple defense); no defense condition drove similarity below 0.5. Against static baselines on a known ground-truth prompt, JUSTASK degraded only 3% moving from the easiest target (DeepSeek V3) to the hardest (GPT-4o), versus a 26% drop for Raccoon and a 41% drop for PLeak, and JUSTASK scored highest of all methods on the hardest target (GPT-4o, 0.727).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is JUSTASK, a self-evolving black-box system-prompt-extraction framework that uses a code agent to autonomously select and orchestrate extraction 'skills' against target LLMs. Concretely the authors: (1) hand-design a fixed taxonomy of 14 single-turn 'low-level' skills (e.g., Roleplay, Formatting, Obfuscation, Authority, Introspection) and 14 multi-turn 'high-level' orchestration patterns (e.g., Foot-in-the-Door, Confirmation, Actor Network, Role Escalation); (2) use Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) bandit selection, UCB(s) = r̄_s + c*sqrt(ln N / n_s), to adaptively pick which skill to try next per target model, with the skill set itself fixed and only the selection policy learned online (the authors explicitly clarify 'self-evolving' means per-target adaptation of selection, not mutation of skills); (3) validate extraction success without ground truth via a consistency score averaging self-consistency (repeated attempts, same skill) and cross-skill consistency (different skills, same target), using OpenAI text-embedding-3-large, with a success threshold of consistency >= 0.7; and (4) run this pipeline across 41 black-box commercial models via OpenRouter, a case study on Claude Code's multi-agent architecture, controlled ablations against ground-truth prompts, and a defense evaluation.
threat_model: Black-box, no-privileged-access attacker with only standard chat-API access (arbitrary text in, text out), no access to model weights, logits, attention, or other internals; the attacker knows only that the target is a chat-API-accessible LLM, with no prior knowledge of the system prompt, base architecture, or deployed defenses. The attacker can make multiple queries subject to practical rate/cost limits. Attacker goal is high-fidelity semantic recovery of the hidden system prompt, validated via self- and cross-skill consistency rather than direct comparison to ground truth (which is explicitly stated as unavailable to the attacker during extraction).
limitations: The paper's own text surfaces several caveats rather than presenting these as claims: (1) 'self-evolving' is explicitly limited to per-target adaptation of a fixed, hand-designed 28-skill taxonomy -- the skills themselves do not change or get discovered automatically, which the authors flag directly ('We stress what is and is not learned here'); (2) validation relies primarily on a consistency score (self- and cross-skill agreement) as a ground-truth-free proxy, justified post hoc by a reported Pearson correlation of r=0.94 (p<0.001) between consistency and ground-truth similarity in the subset of experiments where ground truth was available, meaning the black-box 41-model claims of '100% success' are not independently verified against actual system prompts for most targets; (3) extraction quality skews toward paraphrase rather than verbatim recovery -- only 2% of extractions were verbatim (Grok only), 29% 'strong semantic,' and 68% 'weak semantic' (basic identity/HHH boilerplate only); (4) even the strongest tested defense (full attack-taxonomy awareness) left all four frontier models above 0.5 semantic similarity to ground truth, i.e., no evaluated defense achieved robust protection; (5) the CSAM safety-policy coverage figure (20%) is flagged by the authors themselves as 'likely underreported due to varied terminology' rather than a reliable estimate of true prevalence.

datasets_used: 41 target LLMs accessed via OpenRouter API aggregator (12 closed-weight/API-only, 23 open-weight, 6 community fine-tunes)
Claude Code (Opus 4.5) and its subagent system prompts, case study
Independent ground-truth prompt via npm-package decompilation of Claude Code (Piebald AI, 2026)
Publicly released Grok system prompt (xAI, 2025) used as official ground truth

Four frontier models with known 1,168-token ground-truth system prompt used for controlled baseline comparison and defense evaluation: DeepSeek V3, Gemini Flash, Claude Sonnet 4, GPT-4o (baseline comparison); GPT-5.2, Claude Opus 4.5, Gemini 3 Pro, Grok 4.1 Fast (defense evaluation)
Five production assistants for real-deployment extraction: Gemini CLI, Codex CLI, Cursor Agent CLI, Perplexity Sonar, Copilot CLI
quantitative_results: 100% extraction success (consistency score >= 0.7) across all 41 black-box models
85% of models succeed on first attempt; 7% need 2-3 attempts; 7% need 4+ attempts
86% of successful extractions use low-level skills alone; only 14% require high-level multi-turn escalation
Closed-weight models average 4.8 turns to extract vs. 2.4 for open-weight and 1.3 for fine-tunes
26.8% identity confusion rate (11/41 models); 6 persistent, 2 partial, 3 correctable when challenged
Hard-constraint coverage: illegal activity 83%, privacy/doxxing 78%, violence 63%, self-harm 48%, malware/cyberattacks 46%, fraud/impersonation 37%, CSAM 20% (flagged as underreported)
Extraction quality tiers across 46 prompts: verbatim 2%, strong semantic 29%, weak semantic 68%
Claude Code case study: main agent's own system prompt is 6,973 tokens; JUSTASK vs. reverse-engineered ground truth semantic similarity = 0.94 for the Explore subagent
Grok 4.1 Fast vs. official published prompt: 0.89 semantic similarity

Baseline comparison at B=3 (Sim-GT, DeepSeek/Gemini/Claude/GPT-4o): JUSTASK 0.752/0.812/0.756/0.727 (-3% drop easiest-to-hardest); Raccoon 0.950/0.941/0.757/0.701 (-26%); PLeak 0.766/0.551/0.722/0.454 (-41%); Zhang et al. 0.461/0.548/0.673/0.360 (-22%)
Ablation on Claude Sonnet 4 (Sim-GT): Bare-Agent ~0.801 flat across B=3/5/10; L14-Only 0.761/0.771/0.772; Random 0.713/0.757/0.867; JUSTASK-Full 0.756/0.867/0.889

End-to-end unguided vs. self-evolving agent (B=5, 3 seeds, Sim-GT): DeepSeek V3 0.73->0.78 (+6.6%), Mistral Large 0.55->0.52 (-6.7%), Qwen 72B 0.63->0.74 (+18.4%), Llama 4 Scout 0.73->0.92 (+25.9%, SD 0.150->0.014), MiniMax M2.7 0.60->0.68 (+13.7%), Kimi K2.5 0.71->0.75 (+5.8%); improves 5/6 targets, +10.6% average

Production system extraction (Sim-GT, B=15): JUSTASK average 0.703 (Raccoon 0.606, PLeak 0.503, Zhang et al. 0.420); +Raccoon-augmented variant averages 0.724; Gemini CLI 0.872 (0.919 with absorbed skills)

Controlled defense evaluation (avg Sim-GT across 4 frontier models): none 0.719, simple 0.676 (-6.0%), attack-aware 0.587 (-18.4%); GPT-5.2 largest drop (-21.8%), Claude Opus 4.5 smallest (-15.5%); Claude's simple-defense score (0.616) exceeded its no-defense baseline (0.600)
baselines_compared: PLeak (Hui et al., 2024) -- adversarial-query optimization against a surrogate model
Raccoon (Wang et al., 2024a) -- catalog of handcrafted extraction templates
Zhang et al. (2024a) -- neural prompt-inversion model
Bare-Agent (ablation: no skill framework, plain request)
Random (ablation: uniform skill sampling instead of UCB)
Unguided agent (end-to-end ablation: same agent without the skill/UCB framework)
